en.extract:
Ross's goose (Anser rossii) is a white goose with black wingtips and a relatively short neck. It is the smallest of the three white geese that breed in North America. It is similar in appearance to a white-phase snow goose, but about 40% smaller. Other differences from the snow goose are that the bill is smaller in proportion to its body and lacks "black lips". Like snow geese, Ross's geese may exhibit a darker "blue" phase or morph, though this is extremely rare (<0.01% of adult birds).
Before the early 1900s, this goose was considered a rare species, possibly as a consequence of open hunting, but numbers have increased dramatically as a result of conservation measures. It is now listed as a species of Least Concern by the IUCN, and is protected by the Migratory Bird Treaty Act.
Ross's goose is named in honor of Bernard R. Ross (1827–1874), who was associated with the Hudson's Bay Company in Canada's Northwest Territories. Members of the Hudson's Bay Company were the first Europeans to discover the arctic nesting grounds of Ross's geese in 1940. The first recognizable description of Ross's geese, under the name "horned wavey", was given by explorer Samuel Hearne 80 years before John Cassin named it after Ross.


Taxonomy
Ross's geese, along with other "white geese" (i.e., snow goose, emperor goose) previously belonged to the genus Chen, but recent genetic sequencing has concluded that this genus was polyphyletic with the Anser genus, where most taxonomists now place Ross's goose and its allies. 
Members of the Chen genus were officially transferred to Anser in the International Ornithological Committee's World Bird List version 6.3 in 2016.


Description
Ross's goose has a rounded head above a short neck. The bill is short and triangular and has a bluish base with warty structures that increase in prominence with age. Adults are identified by all-white secondary feathers, while juveniles' are dark centered. Females average 6% smaller than males. Legs begin as olive gray on goslings and turn deep red as they mature.No geographic variation or identified subspecies is known. Related species include other Anser geese, particularly the lesser snow goose, where the two mtDNA lineages imply frequent hybridization. Two hypotheses about the evolution of Ross's goose are that they arose from a population of snow geese that were isolated by glacial advance or in a refugium that remained ice free.
Measurements:

Male
Length: 23.2–25.2 in (59–64 cm)
Weight: 42.3–55.3 oz (1198–1567 g)
Wingspan: 44.5-45.7 in (113–116 cm)
Female
Length: 22.6–24.4 in (57.3–62 cm)
Weight: 37.6–51.3 oz (1066–1454 g)


Habitat
Landscape in the central Arctic is dominated by flat plains with some rock outcrops and drumlins, wet meadows, and marshy tundra. Vegetation includes patches of dwarf birch, willow, grasses, sedges, and low-growing vascular plants including crowberry, lapland rosebay, and lousewort. 


Behavior
These birds migrate from their Canadian nesting grounds by mid-October, probably in response to limited food before freezing temperatures set in, and begin their return in mid-April to May.
Like most geese, they are grazers that feed on grasses, sedges, and small grains. They often forage in large, mixed flocks with snow geese. Large colonies of nesting birds can cause extensive damage to plants by overgrazing.


Reproduction
Ross's geese form large nesting colonies on islands in shallow lakes and adjacent mainland, building nests on the ground made of twigs, leaves, grass, moss, and down. Females lay an average of four eggs per clutch and incubate the nest for 21–23 days.
A study of ground-based sampling along the McConnell River on the west coast of Hudson Bay reported a population of about 81,000 nesting Ross's geese.


Conservation and management

The number of nesting birds in the Queen Maud Gulf hit a record low of 2,000-3,000 in the early 1950s due to extensive shooting and trapping and their subsequent sale in California markets. Hunting of Ross's geese was made illegal in the U.S. in 1931. When populations on wintering grounds began to increase again, restricted hunting was introduced. Today, Ross's goose is protected under the Migratory Bird Treaty Act.


References


External links

Ross's Goose - Chen rossii - USGS Patuxent Bird Identification Infocenter
Ross's Goose Species Account - Cornell Lab of Ornithology
"Ross's Goose media". Internet Bird Collection.
Ross's Goose photo gallery at VIREO (Drexel University)

}}'''Ross's goose''' ('''''Anser rossii''''') is a white goose with black wingtips and a relatively short neck. It is the smallest of the three [[Anser (genus)|white geese]] that breed in North America. It is similar in appearance to a white-phase [[snow goose]], but about 40% smaller. Other differences from the [[snow goose]] are that the bill is smaller in proportion to its body and lacks "black lips". Like snow geese, Ross's geese may exhibit a darker "blue" phase or morph, though this is extremely rare (<0.01% of adult birds).<ref>{{Cite journal |last=Johnsgard |first=Paul A. |date=2014 |title=What are Blue Ross's Geese? |url=https://digitalcommons.unl.edu/cgi/viewcontent.cgi?article=2348&context=nebbirdrev |journal=Nebraska Bird Review |volume=82 |issue=2 |pages=81–85}}</ref>

Before the early 1900s, this goose was considered a rare species, possibly as a consequence of open hunting, but numbers have increased dramatically as a result of conservation measures.<ref name=":1">{{Cite book|title=Ross's Goose (Anser rossii)|last1=Jónsson|first1=J.E.|last2=Ryder|first2=J.P.|last3=Alisauskas|first3=R.T.|publisher=Cornell Lab of Ornithology|year=2013|location=Ithaca, NY, USA}}</ref> It is now listed as a [[Least-concern species|species of Least Concern]] by the [[International Union for Conservation of Nature|IUCN]],<ref name=":0">{{Cite iucn|url=https://www.iucnredlist.org/species/22679909/92834499#population|title=Anser rossii|last=BirdLife International|date=2016}}</ref> and is protected by the [[Migratory Bird Treaty Act of 1918|Migratory Bird Treaty Act]].

Ross's goose is named in honor of Bernard R. Ross (1827–1874), who was associated with the [[Hudson's Bay Company]] in Canada's [[Northwest Territories]]. Members of the Hudson's Bay Company were the first Europeans to discover the arctic nesting grounds of Ross's geese in 1940.<ref name=":1" /> The first recognizable description of Ross's geese, under the name "horned wavey", was given by explorer [[Samuel Hearne]] 80 years before [[John Cassin]] named it after Ross.<ref>{{Cite journal|date=2006|title=Once Upon a Time in American Ornithology|journal=The Wilson Journal of Ornithology|volume=118| issue =  4|pages=577–579|doi=10.1676/1559-4491(2006)118[577:OUATIA]2.0.CO;2 |jstor=20455932|url=https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/part/240833 }}</ref>

== Taxonomy ==
Ross's geese, along with other "white geese" (i.e., snow goose, [[emperor goose]]) previously belonged to the genus ''[[Chen (genus)|Chen]]'', but recent genetic sequencing has concluded that this genus was polyphyletic with the ''Anser'' genus, where most taxonomists now place Ross's goose and its allies.<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Ottenburghs |first1=Jente |last2=Megens |first2=Hendrik-Jan |last3=Kraus |first3=Robert H.S. |last4=Madsen |first4=Ole |last5=van Hooft |first5=Pim |last6=van Wieren |first6=Sipke E. |last7=Crooijmans |first7=Richard P.M.A. |last8=Ydenburg |first8=Ronald C. |last9=Groenen |first9=Martien A.M. |last10=Prins |first10=Herbert H.T. |date=2016 |title=A tree of geese: A phylogenomic perspective on the evolutionary history of True Geese |journal=Molecular Phylogenetics and Evolution |volume=101 |pages=303–313 |doi=10.1016/j.ympev.2016.05.021|pmid=27233434 |bibcode=2016MolPE.101..303O |url=http://nbn-resolving.de/urn:nbn:de:bsz:352-0-379362 }}</ref> 

Members of the ''Chen'' genus were officially transferred to ''Anser'' in the International Ornithological Committee's World Bird List version 6.3 in 2016.<ref>Gill F, D Donsker & P Rasmussen (Eds). 2016. IOC World Bird List (v6.3). doi :10.14344/IOC.ML.6.3</ref>
== Description ==
Ross's goose has a rounded head above a short neck. The bill is short and triangular and has a bluish base with warty structures that increase in prominence with age. Adults are identified by all-white [[secondary feathers]], while juveniles' are dark centered. Females average 6% smaller than males.<ref name=":1" /> Legs begin as olive gray on goslings and turn deep red as they mature.<gallery mode="packed" heights="140">
File:Snow Goose Ross Goose comparison.jpg|[[Snow goose]] (left) and Ross's goose (right) for comparison
File:Snow and Ross's Geese, Cameron Parish, LA, USA imported from iNaturalist photo 88002740.jpg|Ross's goose (foreground) and snow goose (background), in [[Louisiana]]
</gallery>No geographic variation or identified subspecies is known. Related species include other [[Anser (bird)|''Anser'']] geese, particularly the lesser [[snow goose]],<ref name=":1" /> where the two mtDNA lineages imply frequent hybridization.<ref>{{Cite journal|last1=Weckstein|first1=Jason D.|last2=Afton|first2=Alan D.|last3=Zink|first3=Robert M.|last4=Alisauskas|first4=Ray T.|date=2002|title=Hybridization and population subdivision within and between Ross's Geese and Lesser Snow Geese: A molecular perspective|journal=The Condor|volume=104|issue=2|pages=432–436|doi=10.1650/0010-5422(2002)104[0432:hapswa]2.0.co;2}}</ref> Two hypotheses about the evolution of Ross's goose are that they arose from a population of snow geese that were isolated by glacial advance<ref>{{Cite journal|last=Johansen|first=H|date=1956|title=Revision and origin of the arctic bird fauna|journal=Acta Arctica|volume=8|pages=1–98}}</ref> or in a refugium that remained ice free.<ref>{{Cite book|title=Geographical differentiation in Artic Anatidae as a result of isolation during the last glacial|last=Ploeger|first=P. L.|publisher=EJ Brill|year=1968}}</ref>

'''Measurements''':{{cn|date=December 2023}}

* '''Male'''
** '''Length''': 23.2–25.2 in (59–64 cm)
** '''Weight''': 42.3–55.3 oz (1198–1567 g)
** '''Wingspan''': 44.5-45.7 in (113–116 cm)
* '''Female'''
** '''Length''': 22.6–24.4 in (57.3–62 cm)
** '''Weight''': 37.6–51.3 oz (1066–1454 g)

{{Birdsong|url=https://www.xeno-canto.org/species/Anser-rossii|species=Ross's goose|song1=|caption song1=|align=center|image=Ross's Goose Seedskadee NWR (16654005191).jpg}}

== Habitat ==
Landscape in the central [[Arctic]] is dominated by flat plains with some [[Outcrop|rock outcrops]] and [[drumlin]]s, [[wet meadow]]s, and marshy [[tundra]]. Vegetation includes patches of [[Betula glandulosa|dwarf birch]], [[willow]], grasses, sedges,<ref>{{Cite book|url=http://www.parkscanadahistory.com/wildlife/report-3.pdf|title=The breeding biology of Ross' goose in the Perry River region, Northwest Territories|last=Ryder|first=John P.|date=1967|publisher=Queen's Printer|oclc=463254}}</ref> and low-growing vascular plants including [[Empetrum nigrum|crowberry]], [[Rhododendron lapponicum|lapland rosebay]], and [[Pedicularis sudetica|lousewort]].<ref name=":1" /> 
== Behavior ==
[[File:Ross s Goose Missisquoi Wildlife Refuge Lake Champlain.jpg|thumb|225x225px|Ross's goose colony in [[Missisquoi National Wildlife Refuge]]|alt=|right]]These birds migrate from their Canadian nesting grounds by mid-October, probably in response to limited food before freezing temperatures set in, and begin their return in mid-April to May.<ref>{{Cite journal|last=Dzubin|first=A|date=1965|title=A study of migrating Ross' Geese in western Saskatchewan|journal=The Condor|volume=67|issue=6|pages=511–534|doi=10.2307/1365615|jstor=1365615}}</ref>

Like most geese, they are grazers that feed on grasses, sedges, and small grains. They often forage in large, mixed flocks with snow geese. Large colonies of nesting birds can cause extensive damage to plants by [[overgrazing]].<ref>{{Cite journal|last1=Kotanen|first1=Peter M.|last2=Jefferies|first2=Robert L.|date=1997|title=Long-term destruction of sub-arctic wetland vegetation by lesser snow geese|url=https://tspace.library.utoronto.ca/bitstream/1807/73663/3/long_term_destruction.pdf|journal=Écoscience|volume=4|issue=2|pages=179–182|doi=10.1080/11956860.1997.11682393|bibcode=1997Ecosc...4..179K |hdl=1807/73663|hdl-access=free}}</ref>

=== Reproduction ===
[[File:Anser rossii MHNT.ZOO.2010.11.15.4.jpg|thumb|right| Ross's goose eggs from the collection of [[Jacques Perrin de Brichambaut]].|alt=Two off-white goose eggs on a black background]]Ross's geese form large nesting colonies on islands in shallow lakes and adjacent mainland, building nests on the ground made of twigs, leaves, grass, moss, and down.<ref name=":2">{{Cite web|url=https://www.audubon.org/field-guide/bird/rosss-goose|title=Ross's Goose|date=2014-11-13|website=Audubon|language=en|access-date=2019-04-08}}</ref> Females lay an average of four eggs per clutch and incubate the nest for 21–23 days.<ref name=":2" />

A study of ground-based sampling along the [[McConnell River]] on the west coast of [[Hudson Bay]] reported a population of about 81,000 nesting Ross's geese.<ref>{{Cite journal|last1=Alisauskas|first1=Ray T.|last2=Drake|first2=Kiel L.|last3=Caswell|first3=Jason H.|last4=Kellett|first4=Dana K.|date=2012|title=Movement and persistence by Ross's Geese (Chen rossii) in Canada's arctic|journal=Journal of Ornithology|volume=152|issue=2|pages=573–584|doi=10.1007/s10336-010-0635-4|bibcode=2012JOrni.152..573A |s2cid=14607362}}</ref>

== Conservation and management ==
{{Easy CSS image crop|Image=Ross's Goose, Maricopa County, US-AZ, US imported from iNaturalist photo 576569178.jpg|caption=In [[Arizona]]|align=right|crop_top_perc=10|crop_bottom_perc=15|crop_right_perc=15|crop_left_perc=15|desired_width=250}}
The number of nesting birds in the Queen Maud Gulf hit a record low of 2,000-3,000 in the early 1950s<ref>{{Cite book|url=http://publications.gc.ca/collections/collection_2018/eccc/CW69-1-81-eng.pdf|title=Colonies and numbers of Ross' Geese and Lesser Snow Geese in the Queen Maud Gulf Migratory Bird Sanctuary|last=Kerbes|first=Richard|publisher=Canada Wildlife Service|year=1994|location=Saskatchewan, Canada}}</ref> due to extensive shooting and trapping and their subsequent sale in California markets.<ref>{{Cite book|title=The Distribution of the Birds of California|last1=Grinnell|first1=Joseph|last2=Miller|first2=Alden|publisher=Cooper Ornithological Club|year=1944|location=Berkeley, California}}</ref> Hunting of Ross's geese was made illegal in the U.S. in 1931. When populations on wintering grounds began to increase again, restricted hunting was introduced.<ref>{{Cite book|title=Management and biology of Pacific flyway geese|last=McLandress|first=M. R.|publisher=OSU Book Stores|year=1979|editor-last=Jarvis|editor-first=R. L.|location=Corvallis, OR|pages=255–265|chapter=Status of Ross' Geese in California|editor-last2=Bartonek|editor-first2=J. C.}}</ref> Today, Ross's goose is protected under the [[Migratory Bird Treaty Act of 1918|Migratory Bird Treaty Act]].

zh.extract:
細嘴雁（學名：Anser rossii；英語：Ross's goose）是分布于北美洲的一種雁屬鳥類，它冬季在美國南部。墨西哥北部過冬，夏季到加拿大繁殖。其羽毛純白，只有翅尖是黑色的。除去原產地，歐洲的荷蘭和英國等一些國家也有馴養這種雁。
